posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked:
Excerpt from a note on CT:

“The body is thick, heavy and creamy with that much time on lees. The fruit has settled and the complexity has arrived. Bruised apple & pear, a touch of apple cider, pineapple if it were an oil, an array of soft citrus- lemon, lime, orange peel, tangerine, ripe, juicy, yellow peach, some blackberries, marmalade, meringue, cream, malt, some caramel notes, brioche, baguette crust, sea fossils & spray, limestone marl, fine powdery chalkiness w/ speckles of grain, white spice-ginger, withering yellow flowers, excellent acidity, well knitted, perfect tension, balance and a long finish that lasts minutes.”

Kudos to the winemaker who can stuff all that into one 750ml bottle!

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Rob_Isaacs:

Sea fossils? That’s a new one for me.

This is of course complete nonsense in a tasting note.

There are land fossils such as most dinosaurs and plant fossils and many many different kinds of marine fossils both macro and micro. None of them have any flavour or taste.

As a geologist I get a giggle out of the (moderately common) misuse of geologic terms in tasting notes.

Apart from Halite (salt in rock form) very very few rocks have any discerable taste or flavour.

Brodie

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I have often defined two types of tasters and evidently Allen Meadows uses this categorization: structuralists (Clive Coates) and adjectivists (as embodied in OP comment). I am of, and prefer, the former camp.

posts.name: David K o l i n
posts.cooked:
Richard Flack:

Seriously, could even a supertaster distinguish that many elements of flavour?

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
At least they mentioned acidity and texture. The OTT fruit salad critics used to leave that out.

However… ‘excellent’ acidity tells us nothing. It might be spine-tinglingly spiky, or soft and mostly hidden. All that’s saying is the taster liked it, or to put it another way, the acidity gets 95 points

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
brodie_thomson:

Apart from Halite (salt in rock form) very very few rocks have any discerable taste or flavour.

Brodie

and IIRC when people do ‘taste’ wet rock, it’s likely to be organic growth on the rock (moss etc.)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: As a general rule, I think the length/detail of the note should be proportional to the interest of the wine. A tedious, anonymous wine doesn’t need 50 words; for some wines, words aren’t really enough. As far as flavor descriptors go, they may be numerous, but, at least in a professional tasting note, should be precise. In the perfume industry, you have to memorize around 1,500 smells which are all very tightly defined, with reference to particular molecules, and while that sort of precision is generally lacking from wine writing, it should be attainable. Of course, flavor descriptors are no substitute for structure/texture descriptors…

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Of course, flavor descriptors are no substitute for structure/texture descriptors…

this likely comes with some level of experience, but IMO (and i expect most here) structure / texture is far more important than flavor descriptors.

though in perfume, that’s not relevant (?).

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked: Strangely enough rocks can have smells. When you wet gravel on a driveway you can smaell it. When you crack up open a hard igneous or metamorphic rock you can get a gunpowder like smell. There are others of course.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ideally, one doesn’t have to choose, as I think both are important parts of a wine’s identity. And in any case, the distinction is likely a little false: more ripe fruit, for example, gives a particular set of aromas, and also a particular set of textures…

But I cited the perfume industry because I find their precision both impressive and admirable. Only once we perceive the same things and use, if not the same, at least mutually comprehensible terminology to talk about those things, can we most meaningfully talk about wine.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
brodie_thomson:

There are land fossils such as most dinosaurs and plant fossils and many many different kinds of marine fossils both macro and micro. None of them have any flavour or taste.

As a geologist I get a giggle out of the (moderately common) misuse of geologic terms in tasting notes.

You never used the taste test to distinguish brachiopod fossils from bivalves?

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
For my notes I try to capture 2-3 primary descriptors for the nose, 2-3 for for the flavors of the wine, and then I like to describe the texture of the palate and then my impression of the finish. This structure helps me remember the wine when I’m deciding what to pull from the cellar.

I am a fan of how Jeremy Holmes is able to succinctly capture the essence of a wine in his tasting notes. I’d love to have that skill.

posts.name: Howard Cooper

alan_weinberg:

I have often defined two types of tasters and evidently Allen Meadows uses this categorization: structuralists (Clive Coates) and adjectivists (as embodied in OP comment). I am of, and prefer, the former camp.

Agreed. My tasting notes rarely go more than a line or two and if they do I am likely comparing it to another wine, not sea fossils. If I love a wine, I probably will be discussing balance and a long finish, maybe throwing in a comment that Jay Hack would hate the wine.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Call me a Philistine, but I am decidedly in the non-structuralist camp.

I have no problem with someone describing a wine as being evocative of Catherine Deneuve or “Sugaree.”

posts.name: David Westcott
posts.cooked:
I’m late to the wine tasting table and so I continue to learn from you along with many other contributors here. But a bottle of Chanel No. 5 manufactured in 1965 should have identical descriptors as the same perfume made in 2020.

As long as agricultural variables exist how can we ever perceive the same things and use the same terminology to describe a wine?

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
A couple of writers might appeal if you want to dig into this a little:

  • Jamie Goode is a wine writer specialising in wine science. I’ve not read his books, but would be amazed if he doesn’t get deep into the chemical composition / sensory detection side of things
  • Ian d’Agata is an Italian wine writer who has a passion for Italian grapes and also immerses himself in the chemistry (alongside grape identification methods), and has a rare skill to cover technical aspects, whilst still carrying us non-technicians along for the journey. For many grapes/wines, he’ll say what compounds are common for the wine, and how this influences the flavour/aroma.

posts.name: Daniel Schoeff
posts.cooked:
Depends of course on the intent of the description. To inform oneself, to inform others, or to bloviate. Most in this long-winded group are bloviators.

Here’s my litmus test: if you write 30 or 75 words about a wine and I can’t tell if you thought it was good, great, terrible or fantastic… why are you doing it???

posts.name: Victor Hong
posts.cooked:
90 is sufficient.

Oh, wait,……

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My point is that describing aromas, at least as a professional critic, shouldn’t just be free association. A very easy example would be a wine containing a high level of methoxypyrazines. Firstly, you have to be able to perceive that—which will depend on your threshold and your training. And then, the description that follows is grassy/herbaceous/bell pepper. If you say it smells like cumin, you’re wrong. And there are quite a lot of key aromas like this, with a chemical basis, that professionals can train to recognize and differentiate.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

My point is that describing aromas, at least as a professional critic, shouldn’t just be free association. A very easy example would be a wine containing a high level of methoxypyrazines. Firstly, you have to be able to perceive that—which will depend on your threshold and your training. And then, the description that follows is grassy/herbaceous/bell pepper. If you say it smells like cumin, you’re wrong. And there are quite a lot of key aromas like this, with a chemical basis, that professionals can train to recognize and differentiate.

I agree with the general sentiment of this, but in the detail, I dislike the use of chemical or process descriptors to convey a sensory experience. I’ve done this myself many times in the past but mostly try to avoid it now. For example, I would far rather someone say that a wine smells of bell pepper or jalapeño or minty or grassy than saying it smells of pyrazines. That’s partly because the ways in which these compounds interact produce variable sensory experiences, and those different green descriptors are all quite different aromatically. But it’s also partly that there is a tendency among some people, most notably but not exclusively in the sommelier world, to use the language of chemistry to make things sound more clear cut than they are.

I don’t crave a wine criticism that uses the language of perfume chemistry, in part because it would require an audience versed in that language. I don’t even think we have a good enough mutual understanding of wine chemistry to use this kind of language much within the industry. I’ve had winemakers tell me their underripe wines have pyrazines when they don’t, probably because they use “green” to mean “underripe” and at some point came to think that underripe flavors were all caused by pyrazines. Not to mention the sommelier who I heard say that a wine had been barrel fermented when in fact it never saw wood at all. I understood what he meant in a sensory sense by “barrel fermented character”, and I use that term myself for my own reference notes, but I don’t think the average patron at his wine bar would know what to make of that.
posts.display_username: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: M 1 @_U n 5 0 n
posts.cooked:
David_Westcott:

As long as agricultural variables exist how can we ever perceive the same things and use the same terminology to describe a wine?

Add personal experience to the variables. If you’ve never eaten a lychee or gooseberry–and which variety of these–how are you able to make a comparison to these?

I do get irritated by descriptors that don’t make any sense. “Sea fossils”, whether supposedly a description of aroma or taste, is positively ridiculous. But I more commonly see imprecise descriptors like “white flowers” (jasmine? white roses? white heliotrope? white narcissus?) or “red fruit” (red grapes? red apples? raspberries? redcurrants?). If what is meant is jasmine or raspberry, why not say so? “Withering yellow flowers” is what, exactly? A withering yellow sunflower smells very different from withering yellow yarrow. That vagueness of language doesn’t tell me anything except perhaps that the author has only a loose grasp of botany.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
M1a_U:

“Sea fossils”

I’m pretty tolerant of weird descriptors as long as someone is not a professional wine writer because I assume they mean something to the reviewer.

Sea fossils probably means iodine, saline, "Chablis’ like aroma or flavor. If this guy (gal) feels like using sea fossil, good on him.

Besides, if I was a winemaker, I think I’d be happy the reviewer took the time to taste my wine in a very attentive way to pull out all those descriptors.

That being said, if I start using that many descriptors, please slap me… on the cheek not red from having been slapped by my wife first.
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked: I find descriptors such as these completely useless. When I read a wine writer’s tasting notes that are chock full of such comparisons, it seems to me that they are just fiddling with themselves. I want to know about structure, complexity, length, whether there is any noticeable warmth of alcohol, signs of over ripeness, roasted quality, etc. This is what will give me an idea of whether the wine is to my taste and worth buying. Ok, red fruit vs. black fruit would be “allowed”. Would I choose to buy a wine or pass based on a shopping list of descriptors, or simply think that the author is trying to show off?

posts.name: Al1 M ï c h ě l
posts.cooked:
Agreed. It’s like in the movie “Somm”, when IIRC the guy takes one sniff at his glass, identifies the wine as chardonnay, and starts listing 10+ aromas usually associated with chardo. Oh really, you got all that in one short sniff?!?

Other than that, one descriptor that gets to me is “layers upon layers of [red/black fruits, spices, …]”. Yeah, like how many layers? 7, 16, 33?
posts.display_username: Al1 M ï c h ě l

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Phil_T_r_o_t_t_e_r:

I’m pretty tolerant of weird descriptors as long as someone is not a professional wine writer because I assume they mean something to the reviewer.

The goal is to communicate, in words, the taster’s perception of the wine.

If the person uses something imprecise (“white flowers”), literally incorrect (“graphite”), goofy (“sea fossils”), numerical (“94 points”), or un-PC within reason (“feminine”) yet some readers have a sense of what the writer means, then I’m totally fine with it.

It’s about communication and wine and enjoyment, it’s not a college chemistry paper or technical work memo.

I (way too often) say in these threads say, don’t get too critical, demanding or mean about amateur tasting notes. It will just mean fewer and fewer people will write them and share about wine, and that isn’t for the better overall.

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

amateur tasting notes

whatcha said with the quoted text being the key.
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ah, well we agree entirely - so I think you missed my point (perfumers don’t talk about molecules either, unless they’re talking formulae). My point is that the important wine aromas and aroma groups do in many cases have an objective chemical basis, and thus that it is possible to talk about them with the same precision that perfumers talk about smells. That doesn’t imply any need to cite molecules; just some discipline and rigor about what smells of what.

That is not currently the case for most of the wine industry. And if your singed alder is my burnt rubber, for example, it is very difficult for us to talk about what we are experiencing.

reply_to_user.name: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Richard Flack:

It was written by ChatGPT perhaps.

Seriously, could even a supertaster distinguish that many elements of flavour?

As a practical matter that description actually tells me nothing really. It’s like someone banging all the keys on a piano at once. And, speckles of grain, really?

For the sake of serious discussion rather than hyperbolic criticism by others - not by you - of someone else’s description, I do not think that this is a supertaster issue. I have read a lot over the years about how a human can distinguish two, or at most three, different flavors in a wine. However, NONE of that analysis ever mentions that we do not taste one sip of a wine and throw out the rest. If I can distinguish two flavors at a time, why can’t I distinguish five flavors in six sips from the same glass? If I go to a symphony performance, and I focus very hard, I can discern, for example, the sound of the first clarinet. If I then refocus my attention, I can discern the trumpet, and next, perhaps, the cello. If I then sit back and relax, it all comes together as a symphony. Am I a super-listener? No. So why is tasting so different?
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: David Westcott
posts.cooked:
Alas, the more I learn the more imposing wine tasting becomes. And individual tastes come into play as well, whether you are highly trained or not. If I detect and consider as positive the taste of cilantro in a dish and my wife describes a soapy flavor of the same dish, who has defective olfactory receptors?

So far in my wine journey I’ve found it far more useful and economically practical to buy bottles based on descriptors from critics whose opinions align with my tastes rather than buy, taste, and reject random bottles. For me, William Kelley and Jasper Morris guide my choices of pinot noir and chardonnay from France. For the same varietals in the US I use the collective wisdom of WineBeserkers with a nod toward Oregon producers.

Bottom line, it will take a long time for me to have the confidence to post a tasting note. But I enjoy most of yours.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
David_Westcott:

Alas, the more I learn the more imposing wine tasting becomes.

. . .

Bottom line, it will take a long time for me to have the confidence to post a tasting note. But I enjoy most of yours.

This is the concern I expressed above. People enjoy having sport with the foibles and imperfections of tasting notes, but that ends up having a chilling effect on people here and elsewhere, many of whom probably have some interesting experiences to share.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: An excellent point. I assume you mean several sips over a fairly short period of time; as opposed to evolution over the course of an evening which is a separate issue (and is worth describing as such)

reply_to_user.name: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Oh for sure, I’m 100% on board for a more informed and mutually intelligible language of wine appreciation. But the objective chemical basis will always be interpreted through a subjective and genetically diverse set of noses and brains, so I’m not sure how far we can get in that effort.

For example, going back to the perfume world, because it’s one I love dearly, I’m hyposmic to musks. Quite a lot of people are. So precise descriptions about the character of musks and their contributions to perfumes have always completely baffled me. And even as an active perfume enthusiast it took me several years to find the reason for this. I think that no matter the precision of the language, this would have been the case. And most people have something or other like this. My wife smells sulfides and experiences bitterness very differently to how I do, and it took us about 10 years of close coexistence to get a handle on that variation and how it relates to our enjoyment of wines.

This is why I am so gung ho about the application of sensory science to wine consumption - the more we survey people the more we learn about their variability, the better we get at discussing the range of experiences people are having with the same wine. But the more sensory panels I conduct the fewer universal experiences or reference points I find. If singed alder and burnt rubber are two of several identical experiences interpreted through different lenses, maybe what we need is a thesaurus of wine aromatics!
posts.display_username: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked:
How about:
“…an inviting saline note that rises like the froth of waves smashing against the docks on a cold winter seaside morning.”

?? And that’s for a RED wine! (Ian d’Agata)

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Richard Flack:

An excellent point. I assume you mean several sips over a fairly short period of time; as opposed to evolution over the course of an evening which is a separate issue (and is worth describing as such)

Yes, but both are legitimate explanations of multiple flavors. When I get multiple flavors over, let’s say, two or more hours, I will say so in the note to make that clear, but others may not bother. I remember one interesting dinner 20 years ago with some very serious wine. I brought a 1975 Cheval Blanc, there was a magnum of 1989 Haut Brion, a couple of 1982 and 1989 Bordeaux, etc. There was one wine where one of thepeople at the table suggested that he tasted some chocolate. I concentrated on that and got chocolate as well, after not noticing it at first. Was it there or was it the power of suggestion? I do not know.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: I think it’s a fun part of wine tasting when someone calls out a (non BS) thing they find in the wine, and others check and discover it’s there as well. That happens a decent amount in my tasting groups here.

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

As a general rule, I think the length/detail of the note should be proportional to the interest of the wine. A tedious, anonymous wine doesn’t need 50 words; for some wines, words aren’t really enough. As far as flavor descriptors go, they may be numerous, but, at least in a professional tasting note, should be precise. In the perfume industry, you have to memorize around 1,500 smells which are all very tightly defined, with reference to particular molecules, and while that sort of precision is generally lacking from wine writing, it should be attainable. Of course, flavor descriptors are no substitute for structure/texture descriptors…

It’s actually quite a bit higher than 1500. Perfumers (and flavorists) have countless proprietary ingredients via the companies R&D. Botanicals, tinctures, reaction products, specialty distillates, chemicals both nature identical and synthetic etc. etc.

Your analogy to the Flavor and Fragrance industry is spot on.

Tom
